summary: I started working for the Department of Social Welfare in 1950, focusing on aid for the blind. Back in 1929, when the first aid to the blind law was enacted, I was involved temporarily helping with application forms. The Department played a key role in implementing this law and supporting blind individuals across California.

California was the state where much of my work and advocacy for the blind took place. In 1928, California voters overwhelmingly approved a constitutional amendment that allowed the legislature to fund aid to blind individuals. The 1929 aid to the blind law was a landmark moment here, and many organizations and people, including Dr. Perry and Ernest Crowley, worked tirelessly within California to improve conditions for the blind.

Berkeley was central to my education and work for the blind. I attended the School for the Blind there starting in 1918, where I met Dr. Perry who was a major influence. Later, I went on to the University of California in Berkeley. The city was also home to the School for the Blind where Dr. Perry taught and inspired many blind students.

The California Council for the Blind was established in 1934 as a formal organization advocating for blind Californians. It grew out of earlier groups like the California Alumni Association of Self-Supporting Blind, which was founded by Dr. Perry in 1898. This Council played a key role in supporting legislative efforts such as the 1929 aid to the blind law.

entity: California Alumni Association of Self-Supporting Blind
summary: The California Alumni Association of Self-Supporting Blind was founded by Dr. Perry in 1898 and served as the nucleus for later organizations like the California Council for the Blind. This group was made up of blind individuals who had become self-supporting and were active in advocating for blind people's rights. They were instrumental in the efforts leading up to the 1928 constitutional amendment and the 1929 aid to the blind law.
cues: precursor to California Council for the Blind

summary: Raymond Henderson was one of Dr. Perry’s students at the School for the Blind around the turn of the century. He went on to study at the University of California in Berkeley and became a successful labor attorney in Bakersfield. He was closely involved with the blind community and passed away about five years before I spoke about him.

Ernest Crowley was a blind man and one of Dr. Perry’s students at the School for the Blind and later at the University of California, where he earned a law degree. He became a member of the State Legislature in 1936 and was the author, along with Dr. Perry, of the first aid to the blind law enacted in 1929. Crowley served in the legislature until his death in the 1950s and was a key figure in advancing blind people's rights in California.

Dr. Newel Perry was a teacher at the School for the Blind in Berkeley from about 1912 until his retirement around 1945. He inspired many blind students, including Ernest Crowley and Raymond Henderson, encouraging them to pursue higher education and leadership roles. Dr. Perry himself graduated from the University of California in 1896, earned a master's degree, and then went to Munich, Germany, for his Ph.D. in mathematics. He was a pioneer in advocating for blind people's rights and was instrumental in the passage of the 1929 aid to the blind law.

I attended the University of California, Berkeley starting in 1922 after finishing at the School for the Blind in Berkeley. Many blind students, including Ernest Crowley and Raymond Henderson, studied there, often encouraged by Dr. Perry. The university was a place where blind individuals could aspire to higher education, which was groundbreaking at the time.

Chick tenBroek was a younger blind student at the School for the Blind in Berkeley when I was there starting in 1918. He was about six or seven years younger than me and was part of the younger group of students who became very active in efforts to improve conditions for blind people in California. We called him Chick tenBroek and he was involved in the advocacy work around the 1928 initiative and beyond.

Munich, Germany, was where Dr. Perry went to earn his Ph.D. in mathematics after completing his master's degree at the University of California. His time in Munich was part of his advanced education that helped shape his pioneering work for the blind community back in California. It was quite remarkable for a blind man in that era to study abroad and earn a doctorate.

entity: 1929 California aid to the blind law
summary: The 1929 California aid to the blind law was a milestone that allowed the state to provide financial assistance to blind individuals. It was drafted by Ernest Crowley and Dr. Perry and became effective in August 1929. This law followed the 1928 constitutional amendment that permitted such funding and marked the beginning of organized state support for the blind.

entity: Article 4, Section 22 of the California Constitution
summary: Article 4, Section 22 of the California Constitution originally prevented the legislature from granting state funds directly to individuals. In 1928, this article was amended by a proposition that passed with an overwhelming Yes vote, allowing the legislature to allocate money to counties to assist needy blind persons. This amendment paved the way for the 1929 aid to the blind law.

The 1928 California Proposition was a historic initiative that amended Article 4, Section 22 of the State Constitution. It was proposed by organized blind groups, including the California Alumni Association of Self-Supporting Blind, and passed with the most overwhelming Yes vote ever recorded at that time. This amendment allowed the legislature to provide funds to counties to help blind individuals, enabling the 1929 aid to the blind law.
